name: Andromeda Extended
description: Andromeda, daughter of King Cepheus and Queen Cassiopeia of Aethiopia, is a figure of tragic beauty and resilience in Greek mythology. Her story is a testament to the dangers of hubris and the power of divine retribution, but also highlights the potential for redemption and heroism. Cassiopeia's boast that Andromeda was more beautiful than the Nereids, sea nymphs, enraged Poseidon, who punished Aethiopia with a devastating flood and a sea monster, Cetus. To appease the god, Andromeda was chained to a rock as a sacrifice to the monster. It was during this ordeal that Perseus, returning from slaying Medusa, discovered her. Struck by her beauty and plight, Perseus negotiated with Cepheus and Cassiopeia, promising to kill the monster in exchange for Andromeda's hand in marriage. After slaying Cetus, Perseus freed Andromeda and claimed her as his wife. Despite objections from Phineus, Andromeda's uncle and former betrothed, Perseus prevailed, eventually turning Phineus and his conspirators to stone with Medusa's head. Andromeda accompanied Perseus on his adventures, eventually settling in Tiryns. She bore him numerous children, including Perses, from whom the Persian kings claimed descent. After her death, Andromeda was placed among the stars as a constellation, forever immortalized in the night sky, a symbol of beauty, sacrifice, and ultimately, triumph over adversity. Her story is not just one of passive victimhood; it's a narrative of enduring hardship and finding love and strength in the face of impossible odds. She navigates treacherous family dynamics, survives divine wrath, and becomes a symbol of hope and celestial beauty, her legacy echoing through the ages.
subtitle: Chained Princess, Celestial Bride, Heroic Mother
achievements: Survived being chained to a rock
Overcame attempted murder by former betrothed

keyMyths.title: The Boast of Cassiopeia
keyMyths.description: Cassiopeia's hubris angers Poseidon, leading to Andromeda's sacrifice. This event sets the entire narrative in motion, highlighting the dangers of divine wrath.

keyMyths.title: Andromeda Chained
keyMyths.description: Andromeda is chained to a rock as a sacrifice to Cetus. This demonstrates the severity of the situation and her vulnerability, setting the stage for Perseus's intervention.

keyMyths.title: Perseus's Rescue
keyMyths.description: Perseus slays Cetus and rescues Andromeda, leading to their marriage. This act solidifies Perseus as a hero and establishes Andromeda's role as his wife and queen.

sources.author: Apollodorus
